comments: This doctoral dissertation presents a clear, systematic study of neural methods for word and document representation. Major contributions are: (1) a theoretical comparison of common word embedding models (incl. relationships between Skip-gram and matrix-factorization approaches); (2) an extensive empirical study of word-embedding training choices (model family, corpus size and domain, hyperparameters and stopping criteria) across a broad set of intrinsic and extrinsic tasks; (3) a joint character–word training method for Chinese that improves both character and word vectors and benefits downstream tasks such as segmentation and classification; (4) a recurrent convolutional neural network (RCNN) architecture for document representation and classification that combines recurrent context modeling with convolution+max-pooling. Strengths: - Thorough literature review and careful taxonomy of distributional methods. - Large-scale, thoughtful empirical comparisons with multiple evaluation types (semantic similarity, analogy, use as features, use as NN initializations). - Practical, actionable recommendations (e.g. dataset domain matters, iteration stopping strategies, dimensionality guidance). - Proposed Chinese character–word joint training is well-motivated and empirically validated. - RCNN is clearly described and shown to outperform several baselines on multiple datasets. Weaknesses and suggestions: - Reproducibility: while many experiments are reported, the thesis would benefit from explicit release of code, trained vectors, and a complete list of hyperparameters and random seeds to facilitate replication. - Statistical testing: include significance testing on key comparisons (some reported differences are modest). - Ablation/analysis: for RCNN and the Chinese joint model, more ablation studies (e.g., sensitivity to β in joint training across tasks, effect of pretraining vs. end-to-end training, per-class performance) would strengthen causal claims. - Error analysis: qualitative examples of failure cases (e.g., types of words/phrases where models degrade) would provide insight for future work. - Some proofs/derivations (appendix) are concise; clearer notation and step-by-step derivations would help readers less familiar with the area. Overall assessment: this is a substantial, well-executed piece of work with both theoretical and empirical value. It advances understanding of practical choices when training embeddings and proposes two useful modeling contributions (Chinese joint training and RCNN) validated on relevant tasks. I recommend dissemination (e.g., archival publication / posting) and encourage release of code and vectors to maximize impact.
